name: Negation (hindi, wala, huwag)
summary: Say no and not using the three negators: hindi for facts, wala for absence, and huwag for commands.
explanation:
Filipino has three distinct negators, and choosing the right one is essential. Hindi negates verbs, adjectives, and identity statements; it is the general not (Hindi ako guro, I am not a teacher; Hindi masarap, it is not delicious).

Wala expresses absence or not having something; it is the opposite of may (have) and mayroon (there is). So may pera ako (I have money) becomes wala akong pera (I have no money), and may tao (there is someone) becomes walang tao (there is no one). Notice that wala takes the linker -ng before the next word.

Huwag is used only for negative commands, meaning do not. Huwag kang umalis means do not leave. With po for politeness it becomes huwag po kayong mag-alala (please do not worry). Keep these three apart: hindi for facts and qualities, wala for absence, and huwag for telling someone not to do something.
pattern: hindi + verb/adjective | wala + -ng + noun | huwag + command

examples.en: I do not know how to cook.
There is no one in the house.

commonMistakes: Using hindi to mean does not have. Absence requires wala, not hindi.
Using hindi for a command. Negative commands need huwag.
Forgetting the linker after wala (wala akong pera, not wala ako pera).
Saying huwag for a simple factual no instead of hindi.
Pairing wala with may in the same phrase; wala already replaces may.
tip: Match the negator to the job: hindi denies a fact, wala denies having, huwag forbids an action.
